questions.stem: A charge nurse is assigning a newly admitted patient with SIADH to a staff nurse. The charge nurse should FIRST consider which factor when making this assignment?

options.text: Which nurse has been on duty the longest and needs a lighter patient load
Which nurse has the skill set and knowledge to safely care for a patient with severe hyponatremia and fluid restriction
Which nurse requested a learning opportunity with an endocrine disorder
Which nurse has the fewest patients currently on the floor

questions.rationale: The primary principle in nursing assignment is patient safety — the right patient to the right nurse. SIADH involves severe hyponatremia (Na+ potentially <120 mEq/L), fluid restriction, seizure risk, and careful monitoring of neurological status. The charge nurse must assign this patient to a nurse who has the competency to manage these risks safely. Seniority/duty hours (A), learning preferences (C), and patient census (D) are secondary considerations and cannot override the fundamental requirement for safe, competent care. When patient safety is at stake, competency always takes priority in assignment decisions.

distractorRationales.a: The option can leave neurologic deterioration untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "nurse has the skill set and knowledge to safely care for a patient with severe" fits the safer response to the cue "The primary principle in nursing assignment is patient safety — the right patient to the right nurse".
distractorRationales.c: "nurse requested a learning opportunity with an endocrine disorder" commits to a competing cause before checking the dangerous pattern. The cue "SIADH involves severe hyponatremia (Na+ potentially <120 mEq/L), fluid restriction, seizure risk" raises concern for neurologic deterioration, so neurologic safety assessment is needed before diagnostic reassurance.
distractorRationales.d: "nurse has the fewest patients currently on the floor" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "SIADH involves severe hyponatremia (Na+ potentially <120 mEq/L), fluid restriction, seizure risk" raises concern for neurologic deterioration, so neurologic safety assessment is needed before routine care.

questions.takeaway: Charge nurse assignments: patient safety and nurse competency = primary criterion. SIADH = hyponatremia risk, seizure risk, strict I&O — assign to experienced nurse. Never base assignments purely on census or seniority when complex conditions are involved.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A postoperative patient suddenly becomes restless, short of breath, and uses accessory muscles. What is the priority nursing action?

options.text: Assess airway and oxygenation immediately
Finish charting before reassessing the patient
Offer a snack to reduce anxiety
Wait to see if the symptoms pass

questions.rationale: Acute respiratory distress is an airway and oxygenation priority. The nurse should assess and intervene immediately rather than delaying for less urgent tasks.

distractorRationales.b: This distractor shifts care toward delayed assessment instead of airway and breathing intervention. The stem cue "A postoperative patient suddenly becomes restless, short of breath" makes oxygenation or ventilation the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses oxygenation or ventilation; "Offer a snack to reduce anxiety" moves care toward routine care before airway and breathing intervention.
distractorRationales.d: "Wait to see if the symptoms pass" misses the bedside risk. The cue "The nurse should assess and intervene immediately rather than delaying for less urgent tasks" points to oxygenation or ventilation. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.

questions.takeaway: When breathing suddenly worsens, assess airway and oxygenation first.

visualRationale.title: first_action_for_respiratory_distress
visualRationale.caption: When breathing suddenly worsens, assess airway and oxygenation first.
visualRationale.conclusion: When breathing suddenly worsens, assess airway and oxygenation first.

questions.stem: Which patient should the nurse assess first?

options.text: A patient with chest pain and diaphoresis
A patient asking for a blanket
A patient waiting for discharge teaching tomorrow
A patient requesting help finding a charger

questions.rationale: Chest pain with diaphoresis suggests a potentially unstable cardiovascular event and takes priority over routine comfort or nonurgent requests.

distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses circulation or perfusion; "A patient asking for a blanket" moves care toward routine care before circulation and perfusion support.
distractorRationales.c: "A patient waiting for discharge teaching tomorrow" misses the bedside risk. The cue "Chest pain with diaphoresis suggests a potentially unstable cardiovascular event and takes priority over routine comfort or nonurgent" points to circulation or perfusion. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses circulation or perfusion; "A patient requesting help finding a charger" moves care toward routine care before circulation and perfusion support.

questions.takeaway: Unstable symptoms always come before comfort tasks.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: At shift change, the nurse receives report on four patients. Which patient should be assessed first?

options.text: A postoperative patient who is newly confused and trying to climb out of bed
A patient asking for the next scheduled pain medication
A stable patient waiting for discharge instructions
A patient who wants help ordering lunch

questions.rationale: New confusion plus unsafe behavior creates an immediate safety risk and can signal an acute change that needs prompt assessment. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Pain control is important, but this is not the highest immediate risk. - C Discharge teaching can wait. - D Meal support is low priority compared with safety and acute change.

distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses neurologic deterioration; "A patient asking for the next scheduled pain medication" moves care toward routine care before neurologic safety assessment.
distractorRationales.c: The cue "New confusion plus unsafe behavior creates an immediate safety risk and can signal an acute change that needs" suggests neurologic deterioration, so "A stable patient waiting for discharge instructions" would not correct the active problem. Neurologic change needs prompt assessment and safety measures before reassurance, discharge, or routine reassessment.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses neurologic deterioration; "A patient who wants help ordering lunch" moves care toward routine care before neurologic safety assessment.

questions.takeaway: When several needs compete, assess the patient with an immediate safety concern first.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A patient on a nasal cannula becomes more restless and starts speaking in short phrases. What is the most useful next step?

options.text: Reassess breathing, work of breathing, and oxygenation
Remove the oxygen because the patient is talking
Assume anxiety is the only cause
Document that the patient is stable

questions.rationale: Restlessness and short speech can be early signs of respiratory distress. The nurse should reassess the patient before assuming the device is working well. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Oxygen should not be removed reflexively. - C Anxiety may be present, but respiratory status must be checked first. - D This pattern is not enough to call the patient stable.

distractorRationales.b: The option can leave oxygenation or ventilation untreated because it commits to a competing cause before checking the dangerous pattern. "Reassess breathing, work of breathing, and oxygenation" fits the safer response to the cue "**Why the others are wrong:** - B Oxygen should not be removed reflexively".
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave oxygenation or ventilation untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Reassess breathing, work of breathing, and oxygenation" fits the safer response to the cue "Restlessness and short speech can be early signs of respiratory distress".
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses oxygenation or ventilation; "Document that the patient is stable" moves care toward routine care before airway and breathing intervention.

questions.takeaway: Changes in speech and restlessness can be early respiratory warning signs.

visualRationale.title: oxygen_device_reassessment
visualRationale.caption: Changes in speech and restlessness can be early respiratory warning signs.
visualRationale.conclusion: Changes in speech and restlessness can be early respiratory warning signs.

questions.stem: A patient with diabetes is ordered regular insulin, but the blood glucose is 68 mg/dL before the dose. What is the nurse's best action?

options.text: Hold the insulin and clarify the order or follow the hypoglycemia protocol
Give the insulin because the dose is already scheduled
Give half the dose without checking further
Ignore the result if the patient feels okay

questions.rationale: Insulin can worsen hypoglycemia. A low pre-dose glucose requires action before giving more insulin. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Scheduled timing does not override safety. - C Dose changes should not be improvised without protocol or provider direction. - D Symptoms may lag behind the lab value.

distractorRationales.b: "Give the insulin because the dose is already scheduled" misses the bedside risk. The cue "Medication safety comes first when the current glucose is already low" points to unstable glucose physiology. Glucose instability can progress quickly, so medication timing and metabolic rescue come before routine follow-up.
distractorRationales.c: The cue "Insulin can worsen hypoglycemia" suggests unstable glucose physiology, so "Give half the dose without checking further" would not correct the active problem. Glucose instability can progress quickly, so medication timing and metabolic rescue come before routine follow-up.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave unstable glucose physiology untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Hold the insulin and clarify the order or follow the hypoglycemia protocol" fits the safer response to the cue "the blood glucose is 68 mg/dL before the dose".

questions.takeaway: Medication safety comes first when the current glucose is already low.

questions.stem: Which patient should the nurse see first?

options.text: A patient with new chest pressure and diaphoresis
A patient needing help setting up a meal tray
A patient asking when rounds will begin
A patient who wants to know if family can visit later

questions.rationale: Chest pressure with diaphoresis can signal an acute cardiac emergency and must be assessed immediately. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Meal setup is not urgent. - C Rounds timing is nonurgent. - D Visiting questions can wait.

distractorRationales.b: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of circulation and perfusion support. The stem cue "Chest pressure with diaphoresis can signal an acute cardiac emergency and must be assessed immediately" makes circulation or perfusion the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses circulation or perfusion; "A patient asking when rounds will begin" moves care toward routine care before circulation and perfusion support.
distractorRationales.d: "A patient who wants to know if family can visit later" misses the bedside risk. The cue "Chest pressure with diaphoresis can signal an acute cardiac emergency and must be assessed immediately" points to circulation or perfusion. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.

questions.takeaway: Acute chest symptoms outrank routine requests.

questions.stem: After surgery, a patient is drowsy and has a respiratory rate of 8/min. What is the nurse's priority?

options.text: Assess airway, breathing, and sedation level immediately
Wait to see if the rate improves on its own
Encourage the patient to sleep longer
Focus only on the incision site

questions.rationale: Slow breathing after surgery can reflect medication effect or respiratory depression and needs prompt reassessment. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Delay is unsafe when breathing is low. - C More sleep is not the priority if ventilation is compromised. - D Airway and breathing come before wound care.

distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses oxygenation or ventilation; "Wait to see if the rate improves on its own" moves care toward routine observation before airway and breathing intervention.
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave oxygenation or ventilation untreated because it normalizes a finding that needs reassessment. "Assess airway, breathing, and sedation level immediately" fits the safer response to the cue "- C More sleep is not the priority if ventilation is compromised".
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave oxygenation or ventilation untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Assess airway, breathing, and sedation level immediately" fits the safer response to the cue "- C More sleep is not the priority if ventilation is compromised".

questions.takeaway: Low respiratory rate after surgery is an airway and breathing priority.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: Which statement by a patient taking warfarin shows correct understanding?

options.text: "I will report unusual bleeding or bruising right away."
"I can double the dose if I miss one dose."
"I should stop the medication once I feel better."
"A green salad every day is unsafe no matter what."

questions.rationale: Warfarin can increase bleeding risk, so the patient should know to watch for and report bleeding signs. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Doubling a missed dose can be dangerous. - C Anticoagulants are not stopped casually. - D Vitamin K intake should be consistent, not completely avoided without guidance.

distractorRationales.b: ""I can double the dose if I miss one dose."" misses the bedside risk. The cue "Warfarin can increase bleeding risk, so the patient should know to watch for and report bleeding signs" points to bleeding or anticoagulation injury. Teaching items should reinforce the specific medication, symptom, or safety rule rather than inventing a new emergency.
distractorRationales.c: ""I should stop the medication once I feel better."" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Warfarin can increase bleeding risk, so the patient should know to watch for and report bleeding signs" raises concern for bleeding or anticoagulation injury, so patient teaching and medication-safety reinforcement is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses bleeding or anticoagulation injury; ""A green salad every day is unsafe no matter what."" moves care toward routine care before patient teaching and medication-safety reinforcement.

questions.takeaway: Anticoagulant teaching should focus on bleeding safety and consistency.

questions.stem: Which action should the nurse take first when entering the room of a patient on contact precautions?

options.text: Perform hand hygiene and don the required protective equipment
Check the chart at the bedside without washing hands
Bring supplies in and start care right away
Remove the isolation sign if the patient looks better

questions.rationale: Infection control starts before patient contact. Hand hygiene and the correct protective equipment reduce transmission risk. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Hand hygiene is still required. - C Precautions must be followed before care begins. - D Isolation status should not be changed casually.

distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses infection progression; "Check the chart at the bedside without washing hands" moves care toward diagnostic reassurance before infection or sepsis escalation.
distractorRationales.c: "Bring supplies in and start care right away" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "- D Isolation status should not be changed casually" raises concern for infection progression, so infection or sepsis escalation is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: The cue "- D Isolation status should not be changed casually" suggests infection progression, so "Remove the isolation sign if the patient looks better" would not correct the active problem. Infection control or sepsis escalation limits spread and organ hypoperfusion before lower-acuity care.

questions.takeaway: Safety begins before touching the patient.
